candidate_claim: The paper introduces Benchmark Agent as a fully autonomous agentic system for benchmark building.
Benchmark Agent is claimed to support quick benchmark updates for emerging models, new domains, and changing user needs.
In the main evaluation, generated benchmarks achieved about 96-98% human acceptance and UIA scores from 68.54 to 81.48.
The generated benchmarks are reported to show consistent Qwen3.5 scaling trends on three applicable benchmarks from 2B to 27B.
TeleSWEBench is presented as the first commit-driven benchmark for measuring agent performance in telecommunications software engineering.
TeleSWEBench consists of 734 questions with executable unit tests.
TeleSWEBench uses a two-stage evaluation pipeline separating file-localization ability from functional code-generation correctness.
On TeleSWEBench, the best-performing evaluated ASE tools reached up to a 25% success rate for shippable code.
The paper proposes SenseJudge as a human-preference-driven customizable judgment framework and SenseBench as a benchmark from real-world multi-turn interactions.
SenseBench is described as a challenging dataset from realistic human-AI dialogues covering eight common topics.
SenseJudge is reported to score 70.66 overall on LLM-as-a-Personalized-Judge and outperform compared baselines by 10.68 percentage points on average.
On RewardBench, SenseJudge outperformed compared general LLM-as-a-judge methods and was 1.65% behind the generative reward model without training.
The paper claims MLLM judges tend to favor plausible text over perceptually correct answers when visual evidence conflicts with textual cues.
Perception-Judge-Flex-7B is reported to have higher accuracy and lower perceptual-judgment error rates than baseline judges.
The proposed method is reported to improve over Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking by 4% in pairwise evaluation and 11% in batch-level evaluation.
The ablation reports that using PPJD instead of MMPR-v1.2 as training data improves all measured metrics.
The paper introduces TukaBench as a seven-African-language jailbreak benchmark extending JailbreakBench beyond direct translation through four settings.
TukaBench contains 986 prompts per African language across seven African languages, with African-language prompts machine-translated and human post-edited.
Across evaluated models, African-language prompting more consistently increased deflection than attack success rate.
The paper reports lower judge-human agreement for lower-resource and non-Latin-script languages, where off-target model responses are also most common.
The paper proposes RUT-Bench for assessing LLMs under diverse real-world user tool-calling scenarios.
In RUT-Bench experiments, no tested LLM exceeded 40% overall success, and nearly all dropped under more complicated non-ideal user inputs.
RUT-Bench consists of 1,638 test samples across 59 executable tool-use environments in multiple domains.
RUT-Bench's deterministic Python class environments abstract away real-world API friction such as operational deployment variables.
SemRepo is an RDF knowledge graph with over 81 million triples describing nearly 200,000 GitHub repositories associated with scientific research.
The SemRepo construction pipeline obtained 197,566 unique GitHub repository URLs after validation and deduplication.
SemRepo links 11,867 contributors, about 6% of its person entities, to SemOpenAlex author profiles.
In a 20,000-repository SemRepo use case, 46.4% of repositories were classified high risk, 26.9% medium risk, and 26.6% low risk by the paper's risk score.
The study surveys research data policies for 171 materials science journals across 17 publishers using an expanded data-and-code coding framework.
Among the surveyed materials science journals, 98% had a research data policy and 95% mentioned a data availability statement.
In the surveyed journals, code sharing was optional for 70% and required for only 1.8%.
The paper extends prior coding frameworks to cover data and code sharing requirements and applies them to a 171-journal RDP survey.
The paper develops an energy research software metadata schema from requirement analysis and evaluates it through user testing.
The ERSmeta requirement analysis drew on 32 expert interviews with energy researchers.
The ERSmeta schema consists of 86 top-level elements clustered into ten thematic areas.
Participants assessed ERSmeta as useful and relevant, but identified the high number of elements and system complexity as significant drawbacks.
The paper frames AI model selection as an MCDM problem and proposes an evidence-based framework combining automated data collection, a knowledge graph, and MCDM principles.
The paper reports that resources needed to reproduce and extend the work are openly available in a curated Mendeley Data repository.
The GitHub Repository Extraction pipeline crawled 37,176 repositories and selected 4,610 under predefined criteria.
The current ModelSelect implementation is limited to the Python/PyPI ecosystem.
OpenDORS presents 134,352 unique open research software projects and 134,154 source code repositories referenced in open-access literature.
The authors release both the OpenDORS dataset/workflow under an open license and an open-source Python package implementing the construction logic.
OpenDORS v2025-11 is based on repository URLs mined from 2,656,582 PMC, ArXiv, and JOSS publications.
OpenDORS cannot be used to investigate closed-source or unshared research software because its scope is restricted to openly available software.
The Software Observatory is an OpenEBench web portal that consolidates software metadata from multiple sources for research software analysis.
The Software Observatory initially ingests metadata from registries including bio.tools, Bioconda, Bioconductor, Galaxy ToolShed, SourceForge, and Galaxy Europe.
The Software Observatory initially collected over 65,942 metadata records from major Life Sciences registries.
In the Proteomics case study, only 10% of tools reported testing information or explicit dependencies and only 20% provided download links.
ORCE improves calibration and failure prediction on reasoning and knowledge-intensive benchmarks while largely preserving answer accuracy.
ORCE uses a sampling-based correctness-likelihood surrogate to supervise verbalized confidence without internal probabilities.
ORCE aligns the confidence LLM to its surrogate using a Spearman-correlation reward and DPO.
ORCE's practical algorithm uses SC to build preference pairs for DPO rather than directly maximizing expected SC reward.
The paper proposes unsupervised confidence calibration for reasoning LLMs under a single-generation inference constraint.
The method distills offline self-consistency from unlabeled data into a single-pass confidence predictor.
The evaluation covers 36 model/dataset combinations across math and QA tasks.
The method is reported as the strongest unsupervised approach across language, math-domain, and QA-domain distribution shifts.
MLLM token-level support frequently diverges from verbal self-assessment confidence.
The method extracts token confidence and verbalized confidence as complementary uncertainty signals from MLLMs.
The fused confidence method mainly acts as post-hoc recalibration and reduces ECE across most benchmarks.
The fusion method's AUROC and AUPRC-N improvements are inconsistent and model-sensitive.
The adaptive conformal method enables prompt-dependent calibration while retaining marginal guarantees and improving conditional coverage.
Global-threshold conformal prediction for LLMs can over- or under-cover specific inputs despite marginal guarantees.
Adaptive conformal factuality preserves marginal coverage and improves conditional coverage in long-form QA experiments.
In MCQA experiments, the adaptive conformal method is reported as more stable despite high variance from stochastic LLM outputs and prompt sensitivity.
Code language models frequently produce overconfident wrong predictions and underconfident correct predictions.
The paper introduces a framework combining uncertainty estimation, calibration, and tool-based abstention handling for code models.
The paper reports that weighted scaling consistently improves ECE and Brier score across models.
Uncalibrated uncertainty metrics often overestimate reliability for erroneous code-model predictions.
The study evaluates predictive performance and uncertainty reliability for PPG-based BP estimation under ID and OOD settings.
Among the evaluated OOD datasets, Sensors has the largest SBP distribution shift relative to the training distribution.
Uncertainty reliability degrades under domain shift, while GNLL modeling, ensembling, and recalibration improve OOD uncertainty awareness.
The study's OOD analysis is limited to selected public datasets with dataset-specific constraints.
